src_lumi_framework_v3.citation: Lumi HR — HR AiQ Capability Framework v3 (Internal)
src_lumi_framework_v3.notes: Proprietary framework defining six HR AI capability domains, scoring rubrics, and level descriptors. Developed through design partner research with 40+ UK HR leaders.

src_cipd_2025_capability.citation: CIPD — People Profession and AI: Skills and Capability Survey 2025
src_cipd_2025_capability.notes: Survey of 1,247 HR professionals across UK and EU. Covers AI tool adoption, capability gaps, and upskilling priorities.

src_mckinsey_lnd_2024.citation: McKinsey & Company — Closing the AI Skills Gap: Learning and Development in the Age of AI (2024)
src_mckinsey_lnd_2024.notes: Analysis of L&D investment patterns and skill decay rates for AI-related training.

src_lumi_design_partners_2025.notes: Qualitative research with 12 design partner organisations across retail, financial services, healthcare, and professional services. Covers initiative selection patterns, cost actuals, and delivery risks.

src_eu_ai_act_2024.citation: European Parliament — Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 on Artificial Intelligence (EU AI Act)
src_eu_ai_act_2024.notes: Full text of the EU AI Act. Particularly relevant: Article 6 (high-risk AI systems), Annex III (employment and workers management use cases), Article 13 (transparency), Article 14 (human oversight).

src_ico_guidance_hr_ai_2025.citation: ICO — Guidance on AI and Employment: Data Protection Considerations for HR Use Cases (2025)
src_ico_guidance_hr_ai_2025.notes: UK ICO guidance on GDPR compliance for AI systems in HR contexts, including automated decision-making under Article 22.

src_gartner_hr_tech_2025.notes: Market analysis of HR technology adoption rates, vendor landscape, and implementation cost benchmarks.

src_gartner_data_readiness_2025.citation: Gartner — Data and Analytics Readiness for AI: Enterprise Survey 2025
src_gartner_data_readiness_2025.notes: Survey of 800+ enterprise data leaders on data quality, infrastructure readiness, and AI project failure rates.

src_deloitte_workforce_ai_2025.citation: Deloitte — 2025 Global Human Capital Trends: AI and the Future of Work
src_deloitte_workforce_ai_2025.notes: Global survey of 14,000+ business and HR leaders. Covers workforce AI readiness, change management, and skills investment.

src_acas_ai_employment_2024.citation: Acas — Artificial Intelligence in the Workplace: Guidance for Employers (2024)
src_acas_ai_employment_2024.notes: UK employment relations guidance on AI use in hiring, performance management, and monitoring. Relevant for employment law exposure assessments.

src_wef_future_jobs_2025.citation: World Economic Forum — Future of Jobs Report 2025
src_wef_future_jobs_2025.notes: Survey of 1,000+ employers covering 14 million workers. Key data on AI-driven job displacement, reskilling investment, and skills half-life.

src_cipd_people_mgmt_2024.citation: CIPD — People Management and AI: Practical Guidance for HR Professionals (2024)
src_cipd_people_mgmt_2024.notes: Practical guidance on AI use in performance management, recruitment, and workforce planning.

src_ibm_ai_adoption_2025.citation: IBM Institute for Business Value — AI Adoption Index 2025
src_ibm_ai_adoption_2025.notes: Survey of 3,000 C-suite executives on AI adoption rates, barriers, and ROI realisation timelines.

src_pwc_workforce_upskilling_2025.citation: PwC — Workforce of the Future: Upskilling for the AI Economy (2025)
src_pwc_workforce_upskilling_2025.notes: Analysis of upskilling investment, programme effectiveness, and ROI benchmarks across 52 countries.

src_nesta_ai_skills_2024.citation: Nesta — AI Skills for the UK Economy: Mapping the Gap (2024)
src_nesta_ai_skills_2024.notes: UK-specific analysis of AI skills gaps across sectors, with HR and people management highlighted as a priority area.

capability_domains.short_description: The ability to identify where AI adds value in an HR process and design appropriate human-AI handoff points, including calibrating oversight to risk level.

levels.descriptor: Cannot identify AI opportunities in own workflow
observable_behaviours: Does not recognise repetitive HR tasks suitable for automation
Treats AI as a black-box assistant rather than a designable component
Cannot distinguish between tasks that benefit from AI and those that require human judgement

levels.descriptor: Aware of AI potential but cannot map it to own work
observable_behaviours: Can name AI tools used in HR but cannot explain how they fit into a process
Relies entirely on vendor or IT to define where AI is applied

levels.descriptor: Identifies some AI opportunities in own role
observable_behaviours: Recognises 2-3 repetitive tasks in own workflow as AI candidates
Can articulate why a task might be automatable but cannot design the handoff

levels.descriptor: Maps own workflow and flags AI candidates with basic rationale
observable_behaviours: Produces a simple process map identifying AI-suitable steps
Distinguishes between full automation and AI-assisted human decision

levels.descriptor: Identifies opportunities and designs basic human-AI handoffs
observable_behaviours: Maps own workflows and flags AI candidates with justification
Specifies which steps remain human and why
Can describe a handoff point and the information flow at that point

levels.descriptor: Designs multi-step workflows with AI integration for own function
observable_behaviours: Produces end-to-end workflow diagrams with AI/human task allocation
Identifies data inputs and outputs at each AI step
Flags where errors in AI output would have downstream consequences

levels.descriptor: Designs workflows with risk-calibrated oversight points
observable_behaviours: Adjusts human oversight intensity based on decision risk (e.g., higher oversight for employment decisions)
Designs escalation paths when AI confidence is low
Can articulate the EU AI Act implications for a given workflow design

levels.descriptor: Designs optimal AI-augmented processes across the HR function
observable_behaviours: Redesigns cross-functional HR processes to optimise AI/human task allocation
Identifies where AI introduces new risks and designs mitigations into the workflow
Measures workflow efficiency before and after AI integration

levels.descriptor: Leads function-wide workflow transformation with AI
observable_behaviours: Defines standards for AI workflow design across the HR function
Coaches others in workflow mapping and AI integration
Connects workflow design decisions to business outcomes

levels.descriptor: Designs optimal AI-augmented processes for the function and advises peers
observable_behaviours: Designs end-to-end workflows with AI/human task allocation at enterprise scale
Calibrates human oversight points to risk level and regulatory requirement
Recognised internally and externally as a practitioner in HR AI workflow design

capability_domains.short_description: The ability to lead people through AI-driven change: building trust, managing anxiety, communicating transparently, and sustaining adoption beyond initial rollout.

levels.descriptor: Unaware of the human dimension of AI change
observable_behaviours: Treats AI rollout as a technology project, not a people project
Does not anticipate resistance or anxiety in the workforce

levels.descriptor: Aware that AI change requires communication but has no approach
observable_behaviours: Acknowledges that employees may be concerned about AI
Has not developed or delivered any AI change communication

levels.descriptor: Delivers basic AI change communications when asked
observable_behaviours: Can draft a FAQ or announcement about an AI tool rollout
Does not proactively identify resistance or anxiety signals

levels.descriptor: Identifies resistance signals and responds reactively
observable_behaviours: Notices when employees are avoiding or misusing AI tools
Escalates concerns but does not have a proactive plan

levels.descriptor: Plans and delivers structured AI change communications
observable_behaviours: Develops a stakeholder communication plan for an AI rollout
Addresses job security concerns directly and honestly
Tracks adoption rates and follows up with non-adopters

levels.descriptor: Builds psychological safety around AI experimentation
observable_behaviours: Creates conditions where employees feel safe to try AI tools and report failures
Shares own AI learning journey including mistakes
Connects AI adoption to individual career development

levels.descriptor: Leads AI change across teams with measurable adoption outcomes
observable_behaviours: Designs and delivers multi-phase change programmes for AI adoption
Measures adoption, confidence, and sentiment at team level
Adjusts approach based on adoption data

levels.descriptor: Designs change architecture for function-wide AI transformation
observable_behaviours: Defines the change management approach for a major AI programme
Identifies and develops AI champions across the function
Connects change outcomes to business performance metrics

levels.descriptor: Leads enterprise AI change with board-level visibility
observable_behaviours: Presents AI adoption progress and risks to senior leadership
Manages cross-functional dependencies in AI change programmes
Builds organisational capability in AI change management

levels.descriptor: Defines the organisation's approach to AI-driven transformation
observable_behaviours: Recognised as the internal authority on AI change leadership
Shapes culture, values, and norms around AI adoption
Publishes or presents externally on AI change methodology

capability_domains.short_description: The ability to work effectively with AI tools in day-to-day HR tasks: prompting accurately, interpreting outputs critically, and knowing when not to use AI.

levels.descriptor: Has not used AI tools in HR work
observable_behaviours: No experience with any AI tool in a professional context
Cannot describe what a prompt is

levels.descriptor: Uses AI tools occasionally with basic prompts
observable_behaviours: Has used ChatGPT or similar for simple tasks (drafting, summarising)
Accepts AI output without critical review

levels.descriptor: Uses AI tools regularly for routine HR tasks
observable_behaviours: Uses AI for drafting job descriptions, emails, or policies
Recognises when AI output is obviously wrong but misses subtle errors

levels.descriptor: Writes structured prompts and reviews outputs for obvious errors
observable_behaviours: Uses role, context, and format instructions in prompts
Checks AI output against known facts before using
Knows to avoid putting personal data into public AI tools

levels.descriptor: Writes effective prompts and critically evaluates outputs
observable_behaviours: Uses chain-of-thought and few-shot prompting techniques
Identifies hallucinations, bias, and tone issues in AI output
Knows when a task requires human judgement rather than AI

levels.descriptor: Optimises AI interaction for complex HR tasks
observable_behaviours: Designs multi-step prompting workflows for complex HR tasks
Tests and iterates prompts to improve output quality
Understands model limitations (knowledge cutoffs, context windows)

levels.descriptor: Develops AI interaction standards for the team
observable_behaviours: Documents effective prompting patterns for common HR tasks
Trains colleagues in effective AI interaction
Identifies when AI tools are being misused and corrects

levels.descriptor: Designs AI interaction frameworks for the function
observable_behaviours: Defines which AI tools are appropriate for which HR task types
Establishes quality standards for AI-assisted HR outputs
Evaluates new AI tools against function needs

levels.descriptor: Leads AI tool selection and interaction standards at enterprise level
observable_behaviours: Contributes to enterprise AI tool procurement decisions
Defines AI interaction competency standards for the HR function
Monitors AI output quality at scale

levels.descriptor: Recognised authority on AI interaction in HR contexts
observable_behaviours: Shapes organisational standards for human-AI interaction in HR
Publishes or presents on AI interaction methodology
Advises on AI tool design from an HR practitioner perspective

capability_domains.short_description: The ability to assess, plan for, and develop AI readiness across the workforce: identifying capability gaps, designing upskilling pathways, and measuring progress.

levels.descriptor: No awareness of workforce AI readiness as an HR responsibility
observable_behaviours: Does not consider AI capability when assessing workforce needs
Has not conducted any AI skills assessment of the workforce

levels.descriptor: Aware of AI skills gap but has no plan to address it
observable_behaviours: Acknowledges that employees need AI skills but has not acted
Cannot describe what AI readiness looks like for specific roles

levels.descriptor: Identifies AI skills gaps at a high level
observable_behaviours: Can describe broadly which roles are most exposed to AI disruption
Has not mapped AI capability requirements to specific roles

levels.descriptor: Conducts basic AI skills assessment for own team
observable_behaviours: Uses a simple survey or tool to assess AI skills in own team
Identifies high and low performers on AI readiness
Recommends training resources but does not design pathways

levels.descriptor: Designs role-specific AI upskilling pathways
observable_behaviours: Maps AI capability requirements to specific roles
Designs differentiated upskilling pathways by role and level
Tracks completion and measures capability uplift

levels.descriptor: Builds AI readiness into workforce planning
observable_behaviours: Includes AI capability requirements in workforce planning models
Identifies roles at risk of AI displacement and plans transitions
Connects AI readiness to succession planning

levels.descriptor: Leads function-wide AI readiness programme
observable_behaviours: Designs and delivers a multi-year AI readiness programme for the function
Measures ROI of AI upskilling investment
Reports AI readiness metrics to senior leadership

levels.descriptor: Designs enterprise AI readiness strategy
observable_behaviours: Develops enterprise-wide AI capability framework
Connects AI readiness to business strategy and competitive positioning
Advises on AI readiness investment priorities

levels.descriptor: Leads enterprise AI talent strategy
observable_behaviours: Shapes enterprise AI talent acquisition and development strategy
Builds partnerships with education providers on AI curriculum
Represents the organisation externally on AI workforce topics

levels.descriptor: Recognised authority on workforce AI readiness
observable_behaviours: Shapes industry thinking on workforce AI readiness
Publishes or presents on workforce AI readiness methodology
Advises government or industry bodies on AI skills policy

capability_domains.short_description: The ability to critically assess AI-generated outputs for accuracy, bias, fairness, and fitness for purpose before acting on them or sharing them with others.

levels.descriptor: Accepts AI output without review
observable_behaviours: Uses AI-generated content directly without checking
Cannot identify hallucinations or factual errors in AI output

levels.descriptor: Checks AI output for obvious factual errors
observable_behaviours: Verifies names, dates, and numbers in AI output
Does not check for bias, tone, or fitness for purpose

levels.descriptor: Reviews AI output for accuracy and basic quality
observable_behaviours: Checks AI output against source documents
Identifies when AI output is off-topic or irrelevant
Edits AI output before using but may miss subtle issues

levels.descriptor: Evaluates AI output for accuracy, tone, and basic bias
observable_behaviours: Identifies gender, age, or cultural bias in AI-generated job descriptions
Checks that AI output matches the intended audience and purpose
Knows to seek a second opinion on high-stakes AI outputs

levels.descriptor: Systematically evaluates AI output for accuracy, bias, and fitness
observable_behaviours: Applies a consistent evaluation checklist to AI outputs
Identifies intersectional bias and subtle tone issues
Documents evaluation decisions for audit purposes

levels.descriptor: Evaluates AI outputs in high-stakes HR contexts (hiring, performance, pay)
observable_behaviours: Applies enhanced scrutiny to AI outputs that affect employment decisions
Can articulate the legal risk of acting on a biased AI output
Escalates AI outputs that fail evaluation rather than correcting silently

levels.descriptor: Designs evaluation frameworks for AI outputs in HR
observable_behaviours: Develops team-level standards for AI output evaluation
Trains colleagues in bias detection and quality assessment
Connects evaluation standards to regulatory requirements (EU AI Act, GDPR)

levels.descriptor: Leads AI output quality assurance for the function
observable_behaviours: Defines function-wide AI output quality standards
Implements systematic audit of AI outputs in high-risk use cases
Reports AI output quality metrics to senior leadership

levels.descriptor: Designs enterprise AI output governance
observable_behaviours: Shapes enterprise policy on AI output review and approval
Connects AI output governance to legal and compliance frameworks
Advises on AI output governance for new use cases

levels.descriptor: Recognised authority on AI output evaluation in HR
observable_behaviours: Shapes industry standards for AI output evaluation in HR contexts
Publishes or presents on AI bias detection and output quality
Advises regulators or industry bodies on AI output governance

capability_domains.short_description: The ability to identify, reason about, and act on ethical issues arising from AI use in HR: fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy, and employee trust.

levels.descriptor: Unaware of ethical dimensions of AI in HR
observable_behaviours: Does not consider fairness or bias when using AI tools
Has not read any guidance on AI ethics in HR

levels.descriptor: Aware that AI can be biased but has no framework for action
observable_behaviours: Knows AI can produce biased outputs but cannot identify them
Has not considered the employee trust implications of AI use

levels.descriptor: Identifies obvious ethical issues in AI use cases
observable_behaviours: Recognises that using AI in hiring without disclosure raises trust issues
Can name the main bias risks in AI recruitment tools
Has not developed a personal framework for ethical AI decisions

levels.descriptor: Applies basic ethical reasoning to AI decisions in own role
observable_behaviours: Considers fairness and transparency before deploying an AI tool
Knows when to seek legal or compliance advice on AI use
Understands the basics of GDPR and its application to AI in HR

levels.descriptor: Applies structured ethical reasoning to AI use cases
observable_behaviours: Uses a consistent framework (e.g., fairness, accountability, transparency, explainability) to evaluate AI use cases
Can articulate the employee trust implications of a specific AI deployment
Knows the key provisions of the EU AI Act relevant to HR

levels.descriptor: Leads ethical AI decision-making in own team
observable_behaviours: Facilitates team discussions on ethical AI use
Escalates ethically ambiguous AI use cases to appropriate governance
Connects ethical AI decisions to employee relations risk

levels.descriptor: Designs ethical AI governance for the HR function
observable_behaviours: Develops function-level AI ethics policy and decision framework
Implements transparency mechanisms for AI use in HR (disclosure, explanation)
Trains colleagues in ethical AI reasoning

levels.descriptor: Leads enterprise AI ethics for HR use cases
observable_behaviours: Shapes enterprise AI ethics policy for HR applications
Manages regulatory relationships on AI ethics (ICO, Employment Tribunal)
Designs employee consultation processes for AI deployment

levels.descriptor: Defines organisational AI ethics standards
observable_behaviours: Shapes the organisation's public position on AI ethics in employment
Advises board on AI ethics risk and governance
Builds AI ethics capability across the organisation

levels.descriptor: Recognised authority on AI ethics in HR
observable_behaviours: Shapes industry or regulatory standards on AI ethics in employment
Publishes or presents on AI ethics in HR contexts
Advises government or industry bodies on AI employment ethics policy

maturity_dimensions.short_description: The degree to which the organisation's technology infrastructure supports AI deployment: integration capability, data pipelines, API connectivity, and vendor ecosystem maturity.

scoring_rubric.label: Fragmented
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Siloed legacy systems with no integration capability. AI tools cannot connect to core HR data.
indicators: HR data lives in 3+ disconnected systems with no API access
No data warehouse or integration layer
AI tools deployed as standalone point solutions only

scoring_rubric.label: Emerging
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Basic integration exists but is brittle and manual. Some AI tools connected to core data.
indicators: Point-to-point integrations maintained manually
1-2 AI tools with live data connections
Integration failures require IT intervention

scoring_rubric.label: Developing
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Integration platform in place. Most HR systems connected. AI tools can access core data reliably.
indicators: iPaaS or middleware layer managing integrations
Core HRIS, ATS, and LMS connected to integration layer
AI tools can query HR data via APIs

scoring_rubric.label: Advanced
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Modern data platform with real-time feeds. AI tools fully integrated with HR systems.
indicators: Cloud data platform (e.g., Snowflake, Databricks) with HR data
Real-time or near-real-time data feeds to AI tools
Self-service data access for HR analytics team

scoring_rubric.label: Leading
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI-native architecture. HR systems designed around AI use cases. Data platform enables advanced analytics and ML.
indicators: HR systems selected and configured for AI integration
Feature store or ML platform for HR use cases
AI outputs feed back into HR systems automatically

maturity_dimensions.short_description: The degree to which senior leadership has articulated a clear, funded, and operationalised AI strategy for the HR function, with visible sponsorship and accountability.

scoring_rubric.label: Absent
scoring_rubric.descriptor: No AI strategy for HR. AI initiatives are ad hoc and unsanctioned.
indicators: No executive sponsor for HR AI
AI tools adopted by individuals without organisational mandate
No budget allocated to HR AI

scoring_rubric.label: Emerging
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI is on the agenda but no formal strategy. Some senior interest without commitment.
indicators: CHRO or CPO has expressed interest in AI but no strategy document
Pilot projects underway without formal governance
No dedicated budget; funded from discretionary spend

scoring_rubric.label: Defined
scoring_rubric.descriptor: HR AI strategy documented and approved. Budget allocated. Executive sponsor named.
indicators: Written HR AI strategy with 12-18 month horizon
Named executive sponsor with accountability
Dedicated budget line for HR AI

scoring_rubric.label: Managed
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Strategy actively managed with KPIs, governance, and regular board reporting.
indicators: Quarterly board reporting on HR AI progress
KPIs defined and tracked for each AI initiative
Governance committee with cross-functional membership

scoring_rubric.label: Optimising
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI strategy integrated into business strategy. HR AI seen as competitive differentiator.
indicators: HR AI strategy aligned to and referenced in business strategy
CHRO presents AI capability as a business differentiator externally
AI strategy reviewed and updated annually with SME input

maturity_dimensions.short_description: The quality, accessibility, and governance of HR data that AI systems depend on: data completeness, accuracy, lineage, and the infrastructure to store and process it.

scoring_rubric.label: Unreliable
scoring_rubric.descriptor: HR data is incomplete, inconsistent, and ungoverned. AI cannot be reliably trained or run on it.
indicators: Core HR data (headcount, roles, performance) has >20% missing or inconsistent values
No data governance policy
Data lives in spreadsheets alongside systems of record

scoring_rubric.label: Basic
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Core HR data is mostly complete but lacks governance and lineage. Suitable only for reporting.
indicators: Core HR data >80% complete in HRIS
No data dictionary or lineage documentation
Data quality issues discovered reactively

scoring_rubric.label: Governed
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Data governance policy in place. Core HR data clean and documented. Suitable for basic analytics.
indicators: Data governance policy approved and communicated
Data dictionary covering core HR entities
Regular data quality audits

scoring_rubric.label: Analytics-ready
scoring_rubric.descriptor: HR data warehouse with clean, documented, and governed data. Suitable for ML model training.
indicators: HR data warehouse with historical data (3+ years)
Data lineage documented end-to-end
Data quality SLAs defined and monitored

scoring_rubric.label: AI-native
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Real-time data platform with feature engineering capability. Data designed for AI use cases.
indicators: Real-time or near-real-time HR data available for AI inference
Feature store for ML models
Data privacy and consent managed programmatically

maturity_dimensions.short_description: The degree to which the organisation's culture supports AI adoption: psychological safety to experiment, tolerance for AI-assisted decisions, and norms around transparency and accountability.

scoring_rubric.label: Resistant
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI is viewed with suspicion or fear. Employees avoid AI tools. Leadership does not model AI use.
indicators: Majority of employees express concern about job security from AI
No leadership role-modelling of AI use
AI tools avoided or used covertly

scoring_rubric.label: Cautious
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Some openness to AI but significant anxiety. Adoption patchy and driven by individuals.
indicators: AI adoption driven by enthusiasts, not systemic
Employees unsure whether AI use is sanctioned
No formal communication about AI strategy to employees

scoring_rubric.label: Accepting
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI use normalised for routine tasks. Employees understand the organisation's AI direction.
indicators: AI strategy communicated to all employees
AI tools in regular use for routine tasks across most teams
Employees know where to get help with AI tools

scoring_rubric.label: Embracing
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI experimentation encouraged. Psychological safety to try and fail. AI champions visible.
indicators: AI champions programme active across functions
Employees share AI experiments and learnings openly
Failures with AI tools treated as learning, not blame

scoring_rubric.label: AI-native
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI embedded in how work gets done. Culture of continuous AI improvement. External recognition.
indicators: AI use is the default for eligible tasks, not the exception
Employees proactively identify new AI use cases
Organisation recognised externally for AI culture

maturity_dimensions.short_description: The AI skills and capability of the broader workforce (not just HR): the organisation's ability to work with, oversee, and benefit from AI tools across all functions.

scoring_rubric.label: Absent
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Workforce has no AI skills. No training or development in place.
indicators: No AI skills in workforce planning or job descriptions
No AI training available to employees
AI tools adopted without training

scoring_rubric.label: Emerging
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Basic AI awareness training available. Some employees self-developing AI skills.
indicators: AI awareness module available (optional)
Small number of employees with advanced AI skills
No role-specific AI training

scoring_rubric.label: Developing
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Role-specific AI training in place for priority roles. AI skills in some job descriptions.
indicators: AI training available for roles most affected by AI
AI skills referenced in job descriptions for new hires
AI capability tracked for priority roles

scoring_rubric.label: Advanced
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Comprehensive AI upskilling programme. AI capability measured and managed across workforce.
indicators: AI capability framework covering all roles
Regular AI skills assessment for all employees
AI upskilling integrated into performance and development

scoring_rubric.label: Leading
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI capability is a strategic differentiator. Workforce AI skills recognised externally.
indicators: AI capability cited in employer brand and talent attraction
Workforce AI skills benchmarked against sector peers
AI capability investment reported in annual report

maturity_dimensions.short_description: The degree to which the HR function itself has adopted AI tools in its own processes: from administrative automation to strategic analytics and AI-assisted decision-making.

scoring_rubric.label: Manual
scoring_rubric.descriptor: HR function operates entirely manually. No AI tools in use.
indicators: No AI tools in any HR process
HR reporting done manually in spreadsheets
No automation of administrative HR tasks

scoring_rubric.label: Experimenting
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Individual HR practitioners using AI tools informally. No sanctioned deployment.
indicators: 1-3 HR practitioners using AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT) informally
No organisational policy on HR AI use
No measurement of AI tool usage or impact

scoring_rubric.label: Deploying
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI tools deployed in 2-3 HR processes with organisational sanction.
indicators: AI tools in use for at least 2 HR processes (e.g., job description writing, CV screening)
Usage policy in place
Basic measurement of time saved or quality improvement

scoring_rubric.label: Scaling
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI embedded across most HR processes. HR analytics team using ML models.
indicators: AI tools in use across majority of HR process areas
HR analytics team running predictive models (attrition, performance)
AI outputs informing HR decisions with human oversight

scoring_rubric.label: Optimising
scoring_rubric.descriptor: HR function is AI-native. AI embedded in strategy, operations, and measurement.
indicators: AI used in HR strategy development and scenario planning
HR function measures its own AI adoption and capability
HR AI use cases shared externally as best practice

maturity_dimensions.short_description: The degree to which the organisation has formal governance, ethics, and risk management in place for AI use: policies, accountability, audit, and regulatory compliance.

scoring_rubric.label: Absent
scoring_rubric.descriptor: No AI governance. AI use ungoverned and unaudited.
indicators: No AI policy or usage guidelines
No accountability for AI decisions
No awareness of EU AI Act or GDPR implications for AI

scoring_rubric.label: Emerging
scoring_rubric.descriptor: Basic AI policy in draft. Awareness of regulatory requirements but no compliance programme.
indicators: AI acceptable use policy in draft or recently published
Legal or compliance team aware of EU AI Act
No formal AI risk register

scoring_rubric.label: Defined
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI governance policy approved. AI risk register in place. Regulatory compliance mapped.
indicators: AI governance policy approved by board or senior leadership
AI risk register maintained and reviewed quarterly
EU AI Act and GDPR compliance mapped to AI use cases

scoring_rubric.label: Managed
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI governance committee active. Regular audits. Accountability clear for all AI use cases.
indicators: AI governance committee with cross-functional membership meets quarterly
Annual AI audit conducted by internal or external auditor
Named accountability for each AI use case

scoring_rubric.label: Leading
scoring_rubric.descriptor: AI governance is a competitive differentiator. External recognition. Proactive regulatory engagement.
indicators: AI governance cited in annual report and investor communications
Organisation engaged with regulators on AI policy development
AI ethics board with external members

ambition_tiers.short_description: HR professionals who adopt AI tools to improve their own productivity and the efficiency of HR operations. Focus is on using AI well, not leading AI transformation.
ambition_tiers.definition: Embracers use AI as a productivity tool. They adopt sanctioned AI tools, follow usage guidelines, and apply AI to routine tasks to free up time for higher-value work. They are not expected to design AI workflows, lead change, or govern AI use — but they must be able to use AI safely and evaluate its outputs critically.

typical_investment_profile.focus: AI literacy training, tool adoption, basic prompt skills

ambition_tiers.what_good_looks_like: Every HR professional can use AI tools for their core tasks, evaluate outputs critically, and knows when not to use AI. AI tool adoption is above 80% for sanctioned tools. Help-desk tickets for AI tools are declining.

ambition_tiers.short_description: HR functions that actively redesign HR processes around AI, develop new AI-enabled capabilities, and build the change management infrastructure to sustain adoption at scale.
ambition_tiers.definition: Innovators go beyond adoption to redesign. They map and redesign HR workflows with AI integration, build AI change management capability, and develop data and analytics infrastructure that enables more sophisticated AI use. They lead AI adoption within HR and begin to influence how the broader organisation thinks about AI in people management.

typical_investment_profile.focus: Process redesign, analytics capability, change management infrastructure

ambition_tiers.what_good_looks_like: HR processes have been mapped and redesigned with AI integration. People analytics team is running predictive models. AI change management programme is active. HR function is seen as an AI leader within the organisation.

ambition_tiers.short_description: HR functions that use AI to fundamentally redefine what HR does, how it operates, and how it creates value — including shaping the organisation's broader AI strategy and workforce transformation.
ambition_tiers.definition: Transformative HR functions use AI to redefine their operating model. They lead enterprise AI workforce strategy, design AI governance frameworks, and use AI to create new HR capabilities that did not previously exist. They are recognised internally and externally as AI leaders. The CHRO is a visible advocate for AI transformation at board level.

typical_investment_profile.focus: Operating model redesign, AI governance, enterprise workforce AI strategy

ambition_tiers.what_good_looks_like: HR function has a published AI strategy aligned to business strategy. AI Centre of Excellence is operational. CHRO presents AI capability at board level. HR AI use cases are cited externally as best practice. AI governance framework is in place and audited annually.

outcome_taxonomy.short_description: Reduction in time, cost, or headcount required to deliver HR processes.
Improvement in people managers' ability to make better decisions using AI-assisted insights.
Improvement in the performance, retention, and cultural fit of new hires.
Reduction in regulatory, legal, and employment relations risk from AI use in HR.
measurement_signals: Employment tribunal claims related to AI decisions

outcome_taxonomy.short_description: Acceleration in the speed at which employees develop new skills and capabilities.
The proportion of eligible employees actively using sanctioned AI tools.
measurement_signals: Monthly active users of AI tools (% of eligible)
AI tool adoption rate by department
Help-desk tickets for AI tools (declining trend)

outcome_taxonomy.short_description: The organisation's ability to adapt its workforce to AI-driven change without disruption.
measurement_signals: Roles at risk of AI displacement with transition plans (%)

outcome_taxonomy.short_description: The proportion of significant HR decisions informed by data and analytics rather than intuition alone.
measurement_signals: % of hiring decisions using structured data
% of performance decisions with analytics support

outcome_taxonomy.short_description: Improvement in employee satisfaction, engagement, and trust in HR processes enabled by AI.
measurement_signals: Employee trust in AI-assisted HR decisions (%)

personas.short_description: Chief Human Resources Officer or Chief People Officer. Accountable for the organisation's people strategy, HR function performance, and AI transformation of HR.
daily_activities: Board and executive reporting on people metrics
External representation on people and AI topics

primary_use_cases: AI strategy development and scenario planning
People analytics dashboards and predictive insights
ai_exposure_profile.decision_authority: Final accountability for AI use in HR

personas.short_description: HR Manager responsible for a business unit or geography. Manages a team of HR professionals and is accountable for HR service delivery and people management in their area.
daily_activities: Managing HR team performance and development
Advising line managers on people issues
Reporting on HR metrics for their area

primary_use_cases: AI tools for team management (scheduling, performance insights)
Overseeing AI tool adoption in their team
ai_exposure_profile.decision_authority: Accountable for AI use in their HR team and area

applicable_scenario_types: Team AI adoption and change management

personas.short_description: HR Business Partner embedded in a business unit, responsible for translating business needs into people strategy and advising senior leaders on people decisions.
daily_activities: Advising senior leaders on people strategy
Using people data to inform business decisions

primary_use_cases: AI-assisted workforce planning and scenario modelling
ai_exposure_profile.decision_authority: Advises on AI use in people decisions; escalates to CHRO

applicable_scenario_types: Strategic people decisions with AI support
Ethics and bias in people decisions

personas.short_description: Talent Acquisition Specialist responsible for sourcing, assessing, and hiring candidates. High AI exposure due to AI tools in sourcing, screening, and assessment.

ai_exposure_profile.decision_authority: Operates AI tools; hiring decisions require human sign-off

personas.short_description: Learning & Development Specialist responsible for designing, delivering, and evaluating learning programmes. High AI exposure due to AI in content creation, personalisation, and learning analytics.

primary_use_cases: Learning analytics and skills gap analysis
ai_exposure_profile.decision_authority: Designs AI-assisted learning; content quality is HR responsibility

personas.short_description: Employee Relations Advisor managing disciplinary, grievance, and complex employment cases. Very high AI risk exposure due to AI use in monitoring, performance management, and case management.

primary_use_cases: AI monitoring and surveillance tools (high risk)
Sentiment analysis and early warning systems
ai_exposure_profile.decision_authority: Advises on AI use in ER; escalates high-risk cases to legal

applicable_scenario_types: AI in disciplinary and grievance processes

personas.short_description: Reward Analyst responsible for compensation benchmarking, pay equity analysis, and benefits design. High AI exposure due to AI in pay analytics and benchmarking tools.

ai_exposure_profile.decision_authority: Produces AI-assisted analysis; pay decisions require HR/Finance sign-off

applicable_scenario_types: Pay equity and bias in AI compensation tools
AI output evaluation for pay decisions

phase_definitions.short_description: Build the prerequisites for AI adoption: literacy, governance, data basics, and the change management infrastructure. Nothing in Build or Scale works without this.
phase_definitions.purpose: Foundation initiatives establish the conditions for everything that follows. They address the most common failure modes: low AI literacy leading to misuse, absent governance leading to regulatory exposure, and poor data quality blocking analytics. Foundation is not glamorous — it is the work that makes the rest possible.
placement_criteria: Initiative requires no prior AI capability in the workforce
Initiative addresses a prerequisite for higher-phase initiatives (literacy, data, governance)
Initiative can be delivered without significant IT infrastructure change
Initiative has low regulatory risk (EU AI Act: none or low)
Initiative complexity: low

phase_definitions.short_description: Deploy AI tools in HR processes, build analytics capability, and establish the operating model for AI-assisted HR. Requires Foundation to be in place.
phase_definitions.purpose: Build initiatives deploy AI into HR processes for the first time at scale. They require the literacy and governance infrastructure from Foundation. The focus is on process automation, basic analytics, and the change management to sustain adoption. Build initiatives typically have medium complexity and require IT involvement.
placement_criteria: Initiative requires basic AI literacy in the workforce (Foundation complete)
Initiative deploys AI tools into 1-2 HR processes
Initiative requires IT involvement but not major infrastructure change
Initiative has medium regulatory risk (EU AI Act: low or medium)
Initiative complexity: medium

phase_definitions.short_description: Expand AI across the HR function, develop predictive analytics, and begin to use AI for strategic HR decisions. Requires Build to be operational.
phase_definitions.purpose: Scale initiatives expand AI from individual processes to the HR function as a whole. They require the data infrastructure and analytics capability from Build. The focus is on predictive analytics, cross-process AI integration, and using AI to inform strategic HR decisions. Scale initiatives are complex and require significant IT and data infrastructure.
placement_criteria: Initiative requires operational AI tools and analytics capability (Build complete)
Initiative spans multiple HR processes or requires cross-functional data
Initiative requires significant data infrastructure (data warehouse, ML platform)
Initiative has high regulatory risk or involves high-risk AI use cases
Initiative complexity: high

phase_definitions.short_description: Continuously improve AI performance, expand to new use cases, and build the AI Centre of Excellence that sustains the capability long-term.
phase_definitions.purpose: Optimise initiatives are the long-term capability builders. They assume AI is embedded across HR and focus on continuous improvement, new use case development, and building the organisational infrastructure to sustain AI capability. They are typically Transformative-tier initiatives.
placement_criteria: Initiative requires AI embedded across the HR function (Scale complete)
Initiative builds long-term organisational capability (CoE, governance, measurement)
Initiative has a 24+ month horizon
Initiative is Transformative-tier ambition
Initiative complexity: very high

ai_literacy_programme.short_description: Structured curriculum building AI fluency across the HR function. Covers AI fundamentals, prompt engineering, output evaluation, and ethics. Delivered as blended learning (online modules + facilitated workshops).

cost.caveat: Excludes internal headcount, vendor licensing, and change management overhead.

typical_risks.statement: Voluntary uptake stalls below 30% without manager mandate
typical_risks.mitigation: Tie completion to manager-level OKRs and team capability scorecards

typical_risks.statement: Skills decay without applied practice within 90 days
typical_risks.mitigation: Pair training with live AI tool rollout in same quarter

typical_outcomes: AI fluency uplift across function
Reduced help-desk tickets for AI tools
Higher initiative completion rates downstream

quantified_value.primary_metric: L&D efficiency gain per HR FTE

ai_ethics_governance_framework.short_description: Development and implementation of an HR-specific AI ethics and governance framework: acceptable use policy, decision accountability matrix, audit process, and regulatory compliance mapping (EU AI Act, GDPR, employment law).

cost.caveat: Excludes legal counsel fees for regulatory review. Budget £5-15k separately for legal.

typical_risks.statement: Framework becomes outdated as EU AI Act implementation guidance evolves
typical_risks.mitigation: Build in annual review cycle aligned to regulatory calendar

typical_risks.statement: Framework approved but not embedded in manager decision-making
typical_risks.mitigation: Mandate governance training for all people managers; include in manager induction

typical_outcomes: Regulatory compliance for EU AI Act and GDPR HR use cases
Clear accountability for AI decisions
Reduced employment tribunal risk from AI-assisted decisions

prompt_engineering_for_hr.short_description: Targeted upskilling in prompt engineering for HR-specific use cases: job description writing, policy drafting, candidate communication, performance feedback, and HR analytics queries. Includes a library of tested HR prompt templates.

cost.caveat: Excludes cost of AI tool licences. Assumes existing AI tool access.

typical_risks.statement: Prompt templates become outdated as AI models update
typical_risks.mitigation: Assign a prompt library owner; review templates quarterly

typical_outcomes: Measurable improvement in AI output quality for HR tasks
Reduced time spent editing AI-generated content
Shared prompt library reducing duplication of effort

ai_acceptable_use_policy.display_name: AI Acceptable Use Policy for HR
ai_acceptable_use_policy.short_description: Development and communication of a clear policy governing what AI tools HR professionals can use, for what purposes, with what data, and under what oversight conditions. Includes data classification guidance and prohibited use cases.

cost.caveat: Excludes legal review. Budget £3-8k for legal counsel sign-off.

typical_risks.statement: Policy published but not enforced; shadow AI use continues
typical_risks.mitigation: Pair policy with manager training and clear escalation process for violations

typical_outcomes: Clear guidance for HR professionals on AI tool use
Reduced risk of data breaches from unsanctioned AI tool use
Foundation for AI governance programme

hr_data_quality_audit.short_description: Systematic audit of core HR data quality (completeness, accuracy, consistency, lineage) across HRIS, ATS, and LMS. Produces a prioritised remediation plan and data governance baseline required for analytics and AI initiatives.

cost.caveat: Audit cost only. Remediation is a separate workstream; budget 2-5x audit cost for remediation.

typical_risks.statement: Data quality issues are more extensive than anticipated, extending timeline
typical_risks.mitigation: Phase remediation by priority: fix data required for first AI use case first

typical_risks.statement: Unclear data ownership delays remediation decisions
typical_risks.mitigation: Assign data stewards before audit begins; escalation path to CHRO

typical_outcomes: Baseline data quality score for HR data
Prioritised remediation plan
Data governance policy and stewardship model

ai_change_management_programme.short_description: Structured programme to build change management capability for AI adoption: stakeholder mapping, communication planning, resistance management, AI champions network, and adoption measurement framework.

cost.caveat: Excludes cost of change management tools and platforms. Assumes internal change lead.

typical_risks.statement: AI change programme competes with other transformation initiatives
typical_risks.mitigation: Integrate AI change into existing transformation governance; avoid parallel workstreams

typical_risks.statement: AI champions take on too much without recognition or time allocation
typical_risks.mitigation: Formalise champion role with time allocation (10-15% of role) and recognition

typical_outcomes: Structured AI adoption communication plan
Active AI champions network
Measurable improvement in AI adoption rates

bias_monitoring_and_auditing.short_description: Implementation of ongoing bias monitoring and algorithmic auditing for AI tools used in HR decisions. Covers audit methodology, tooling, frequency, reporting, and remediation process. Required for EU AI Act compliance for high-risk AI use cases.

cost.caveat: Annual audit cost. Includes external auditor fees (£15-40k/year). Excludes remediation.

typical_risks.statement: Audit identifies bias but remediation is not resourced or actioned
typical_risks.mitigation: Pre-agree remediation budget and accountability before first audit

typical_risks.statement: AI tool vendors resist providing model access for auditing
typical_risks.mitigation: Include audit access rights in vendor contracts; use proxy auditing methods if access denied

typical_outcomes: EU AI Act compliance for high-risk HR AI use cases
Documented bias audit trail for employment tribunal defence
Reduced risk of discriminatory AI outcomes

hr_process_automation.short_description: Automation of high-volume, rules-based HR processes using AI and workflow tools: onboarding task orchestration, leaver process, absence management, and policy query routing. Reduces administrative burden and improves consistency.

cost.caveat: Excludes HRIS configuration costs and vendor implementation fees. Budget 30-50% additional for IT delivery.

typical_risks.statement: Process complexity is underestimated; automation scope expands
typical_risks.mitigation: Complete process mapping before committing to automation scope; use MoSCoW prioritisation

typical_risks.statement: Automated processes fail on edge cases, requiring manual intervention
typical_risks.mitigation: Design exception handling and escalation paths before go-live

typical_outcomes: 30-50% reduction in HR admin time for automated processes
Improved process consistency and compliance
Faster employee query resolution

qualitative_value: Freed HR capacity for strategic work

ai_assisted_job_descriptions.short_description: Deployment of AI tools to generate, improve, and bias-check job descriptions. Includes prompt templates, bias checking workflow, and integration with ATS. Reduces time-to-post and improves job description quality and inclusivity.

cost.caveat: Excludes AI tool licensing (budget £5-20k/year). Assumes ATS integration is in scope.

typical_risks.statement: AI generates job descriptions with gender-coded or exclusionary language
typical_risks.mitigation: Implement mandatory bias check step before publication; use validated bias detection tool

typical_outcomes: 40-60% reduction in time to write and approve job descriptions
Measurable improvement in job description inclusivity scores
Improved candidate quality through clearer role requirements

hr_chatbot_employee_queries.short_description: Deployment of an AI-powered chatbot to handle routine employee HR queries: policy questions, leave balances, payroll queries, and process guidance. Reduces HR help-desk volume and improves employee experience.

cost.caveat: Includes chatbot platform, integration, and content development. Excludes ongoing maintenance (budget 15-20% of implementation cost annually).

typical_risks.statement: Employees distrust chatbot and continue to contact HR directly
typical_risks.mitigation: Communicate chatbot capabilities clearly; ensure easy escalation to human HR

typical_risks.statement: Chatbot knowledge base becomes outdated as policies change
typical_risks.mitigation: Assign knowledge base owner; integrate with policy management system

typical_outcomes: 30-50% reduction in HR help-desk volume for routine queries
24/7 employee query resolution
Improved employee satisfaction with HR services

qualitative_value: HR team focus on complex queries

people_analytics_dashboard.short_description: Implementation of a people analytics dashboard providing HR and business leaders with real-time visibility of key workforce metrics: headcount, attrition, absence, diversity, and performance. Foundation for predictive analytics in Scale phase.

cost.caveat: Excludes data warehouse costs (budget separately). Assumes HRIS data is clean (see HR Data Quality Audit).

typical_risks.statement: Dashboard quality limited by underlying data quality
typical_risks.mitigation: Complete HR Data Quality Audit before dashboard build; publish data quality caveats

typical_risks.statement: Managers do not use dashboard; revert to ad hoc HR requests
typical_risks.mitigation: Embed dashboard in manager induction; tie to manager OKRs

typical_outcomes: Real-time workforce visibility for HR and business leaders
Reduction in ad hoc HR data requests
Foundation for predictive analytics

quantified_value.primary_metric: HR reporting time saved per FTE

ai_assisted_performance_feedback.short_description: AI tools to help managers write higher-quality, more specific performance feedback: prompt templates, bias detection, and feedback quality scoring. Improves feedback quality and reduces time burden on managers.

cost.caveat: Excludes performance management platform costs. Assumes existing PM system.

typical_risks.statement: AI-assisted feedback becomes formulaic and loses individual specificity
typical_risks.mitigation: Train managers to use AI as a starting point, not a final output; measure feedback quality

typical_risks.statement: AI feedback tools amplify existing performance rating biases
typical_risks.mitigation: Implement bias audit of feedback outputs quarterly; include in bias monitoring programme

typical_outcomes: Measurable improvement in feedback quality scores
Reduction in time managers spend writing performance feedback
More consistent feedback across the organisation

quantified_value.primary_metric: Manager time saved per performance cycle

ai_tool_adoption_programme.short_description: Structured programme to drive adoption of sanctioned AI tools across the HR function: tool selection, onboarding, usage tracking, and continuous improvement. Includes AI champions network and adoption measurement.

cost.caveat: Excludes AI tool licensing costs. Assumes AI Literacy Programme is complete.

typical_risks.statement: Multiple unsanctioned AI tools in use alongside official tools
typical_risks.mitigation: Publish approved tool list; IT to block unsanctioned AI tools on corporate devices

typical_outcomes: 80%+ adoption rate for sanctioned AI tools within 6 months
Measurable reduction in time spent on AI tool help requests
Active AI champions network

automated_onboarding_orchestration.short_description: AI-orchestrated onboarding workflow: automated task assignment, progress tracking, personalised content delivery, and manager nudges. Reduces time-to-productivity and improves new hire experience.

cost.caveat: Excludes HRIS and LMS integration costs. Assumes existing onboarding platform.

typical_risks.statement: Personalised content delivery limited by incomplete new hire data
typical_risks.mitigation: Collect role, location, and team data at offer stage to enable personalisation

typical_outcomes: 20-30% reduction in time-to-productivity for new hires
Improved new hire satisfaction scores
Reduction in onboarding admin for HR team

quantified_value.primary_metric: Admin time saved per new hire + productivity ramp improvement

ai_assisted_cv_screening.short_description: Deployment of AI tools to screen and rank CVs against job requirements. Includes bias audit, transparency disclosure to candidates, and human review of AI rankings before shortlisting. High regulatory risk — requires governance framework.

cost.caveat: Excludes ATS vendor costs. Legal review of candidate disclosure required (budget £5-10k).

typical_risks.statement: AI screening tool produces discriminatory shortlists
typical_risks.mitigation: Mandatory bias audit before go-live; ongoing quarterly monitoring; human review of all AI rankings

typical_risks.statement: Candidates object to AI screening and withdraw applications
typical_risks.mitigation: Transparent disclosure of AI use in recruitment; provide opt-out or human review option

typical_outcomes: 40-60% reduction in time-to-shortlist
Improved shortlist quality through consistent criteria application
Documented audit trail for equal opportunities compliance

quantified_value.primary_metric: Recruiter screening time saved per hire

predictive_attrition_modelling.short_description: ML model predicting employee attrition risk at individual and team level. Integrates with people analytics dashboard to surface at-risk employees and trigger manager interventions. Requires clean historical HR data.

cost.caveat: Includes model build, validation, and dashboard integration. Excludes data warehouse costs. Annual model refresh: budget 20-30% of build cost.

typical_risks.statement: HR acts on attrition predictions without understanding model confidence intervals
typical_risks.mitigation: Train HR team on model limitations; display confidence intervals in dashboard

typical_risks.statement: Employees identified as flight risks are treated differently, accelerating departure
typical_risks.mitigation: Restrict attrition risk data to HR and direct manager; strict data access controls

typical_outcomes: 15-25% reduction in regrettable attrition
Earlier intervention for at-risk employees
Improved workforce planning accuracy

skills_intelligence_platform.short_description: AI-powered platform mapping current workforce skills, identifying gaps against future role requirements, and generating personalised development recommendations. Integrates with LMS, HRIS, and workforce planning tools.

cost.caveat: Platform build and integration only. Excludes content development and LMS costs. Annual platform maintenance: 15-20% of build cost.

typical_risks.statement: Skills taxonomy becomes outdated as AI transforms job requirements
typical_risks.mitigation: Build quarterly taxonomy review into operating model; use external skills data feeds

typical_risks.statement: Employees object to skills profiling without clear consent and benefit
typical_risks.mitigation: Transparent communication of skills platform purpose and employee benefits; opt-in for sensitive skills data

typical_outcomes: Real-time visibility of workforce skills and gaps
Personalised development recommendations for all employees
Improved internal mobility and talent retention

quantified_value.primary_metric: Internal mobility rate improvement (external hire cost avoidance)

workforce_scenario_planning.short_description: AI modelling of workforce scenarios: headcount planning under different business growth assumptions, AI displacement impact modelling, and skills gap projection. Enables proactive workforce strategy rather than reactive headcount management.

cost.caveat: Includes modelling platform and integration with Finance planning tools. Excludes data infrastructure costs.

typical_risks.statement: Scenario models used as fact rather than as planning tools with assumptions
typical_risks.mitigation: Document all model assumptions; present scenarios with confidence ranges, not point estimates

typical_outcomes: Proactive workforce planning aligned to business scenarios
Quantified AI displacement risk with transition plans
Improved Finance-HR alignment on headcount planning

ai_powered_learning_personalisation.short_description: AI personalisation layer on the LMS: adaptive learning paths based on skills gaps, role, and learning history. AI content curation and recommendation engine. Improves learning effectiveness and reduces time-to-competency.

cost.caveat: Excludes LMS platform costs and content development. Assumes existing LMS with API access.

typical_risks.statement: AI recommendations are low quality due to sparse learning history data
typical_risks.mitigation: Seed learning history with skills assessment data; allow learner feedback on recommendations

typical_outcomes: 20-30% improvement in learning completion rates
Measurable reduction in time-to-competency for priority skills
Higher learner satisfaction with development experience

sources: Josh Bersin Learning in the Flow of Work 2024

ai_pay_equity_analysis.short_description: AI-powered analysis of pay equity across gender, ethnicity, and other protected characteristics. Identifies unexplained pay gaps, models remediation scenarios, and produces regulatory reporting outputs.

cost.caveat: Annual analysis cost. Excludes pay remediation budget (typically 0.5-2% of payroll for identified gaps).

typical_risks.statement: Ethnicity and other protected characteristic data is incomplete, limiting analysis
typical_risks.mitigation: Run data completeness campaign before analysis; be transparent about data limitations in reporting

typical_risks.statement: Pay equity analysis creates legal exposure if not conducted under legal privilege
typical_risks.mitigation: Conduct initial analysis under legal privilege; engage employment law counsel before commencing

typical_outcomes: Quantified pay equity position with statistical confidence
Prioritised remediation plan for identified gaps
Regulatory reporting compliance (gender pay gap, ethnicity pay gap)

ai_centre_of_excellence.short_description: Establishment of a dedicated HR AI Centre of Excellence: team structure, operating model, governance, and capability development programme. The CoE owns AI strategy, vendor relationships, and capability standards for the HR function.

cost.caveat: Year 1 setup cost including headcount. Ongoing annual cost: 60-80% of setup cost. Excludes technology costs.

typical_risks.statement: CoE becomes isolated from business and loses relevance
typical_risks.mitigation: Embed CoE members in business units; measure CoE impact on business outcomes

typical_outcomes: Single point of accountability for HR AI strategy and delivery
Consistent AI capability standards across the HR function
Accelerated AI initiative delivery through shared expertise

hr_ai_operating_model_redesign.short_description: Fundamental redesign of the HR operating model to embed AI: role redesign, team structure, decision rights, and capability requirements. Defines what HR professionals do differently in an AI-enabled function.

cost.caveat: Design and implementation cost. Excludes redundancy costs if role reduction is in scope.

typical_risks.statement: Operating model redesign creates anxiety about job security
typical_risks.mitigation: Communicate redesign as role enhancement, not reduction; involve HR team in design

typical_outcomes: Clearly defined AI-enabled HR roles and capabilities
Reduced duplication and improved HR service quality
HR function positioned as AI leader in the organisation

quantified_value.primary_metric: HR operational cost reduction through restructuring

ai_governance_maturity_programme.short_description: Multi-year programme to advance AI governance maturity from defined to leading: enhanced audit capability, proactive regulatory engagement, external AI ethics board, and governance reporting in annual report.

cost.caveat: Annual programme cost. Includes external ethics board member fees and regulatory engagement costs.

typical_risks.statement: Regulatory requirements change faster than governance programme can adapt
typical_risks.mitigation: Subscribe to regulatory monitoring service; build flexibility into governance framework

typical_outcomes: AI governance maturity at Level 4-5 (Managed to Leading)
External recognition for AI governance practice
Proactive regulatory compliance ahead of enforcement

manager_ai_coaching_toolkit.short_description: AI-assisted toolkit for people managers: AI-generated coaching prompts, team performance insights, development conversation guides, and wellbeing signal monitoring. Improves manager effectiveness and reduces time burden of people management.

cost.caveat: Excludes manager coaching platform costs. Assumes existing performance management system.

typical_risks.statement: Wellbeing signal monitoring perceived as surveillance by employees
typical_risks.mitigation: Transparent communication of monitoring scope; employee opt-in for wellbeing signals

typical_outcomes: Measurable improvement in manager effectiveness scores
Higher quality performance and development conversations
Earlier identification of team wellbeing issues

quantified_value.primary_metric: Manager time saved on coaching and development conversations

ai_talent_marketplace.short_description: AI-powered internal mobility platform matching employees to open roles, projects, mentors, and stretch assignments based on skills, aspirations, and business need. Reduces external hiring costs and improves retention by surfacing internal career paths that employees would otherwise miss.

cost.caveat: Platform build and HRIS integration. Excludes change management and adoption programme. Requires skills intelligence platform as a prerequisite.

typical_risks.statement: Managers block internal moves to protect team stability, undermining marketplace adoption
typical_risks.mitigation: Embed internal mobility metrics in manager performance framework; executive sponsorship required

typical_risks.statement: Matching algorithm perpetuates historical patterns of who gets development opportunities
typical_risks.mitigation: Audit matching outcomes quarterly by protected characteristic; publish equity metrics

typical_outcomes: 20-35% reduction in external hiring for roles that could be filled internally
Measurable improvement in internal mobility rates
Higher employee engagement scores linked to career development

quantified_value.primary_metric: External hire cost avoidance through internal mobility

ai_performance_calibration.short_description: AI tool supporting performance calibration sessions: surfaces rating distribution anomalies, flags potential bias patterns (gender, ethnicity, tenure), and provides managers with evidence-based talking points. Does not replace human calibration — it prepares and informs it.

cost.caveat: Annual licence and configuration. Requires clean performance data with at least 2 years of history for bias detection to be statistically meaningful.

typical_risks.statement: Managers defer to AI flags rather than exercising independent judgement
typical_risks.mitigation: Train managers that AI output is a prompt for discussion, not a verdict; document human decision rationale

typical_risks.statement: AI trained on historical ratings replicates past discriminatory patterns
typical_risks.mitigation: Audit training data for historical bias before deployment; ongoing output monitoring

typical_outcomes: Measurable reduction in rating distribution variance across managers
Documented evidence of bias-aware calibration process
Improved employee perception of performance fairness

quantified_value.primary_metric: Calibration meeting time saved per employee

ai_employee_listening.short_description: Continuous employee listening using AI to analyse pulse survey data, exit interview themes, and open-text feedback at scale. Surfaces emerging themes, sentiment trends, and leading indicators of engagement risk — without requiring manual thematic analysis.

cost.caveat: Annual platform licence. Excludes survey design and change management costs. Requires minimum 50 respondents per segment for statistically meaningful analysis.

typical_risks.statement: Employees perceive continuous listening as surveillance, reducing candour
typical_risks.mitigation: Transparent communication of data use; aggregate-only reporting; no individual attribution

typical_risks.statement: Listening data collected but not acted on, eroding employee trust
typical_risks.mitigation: Publish listening-to-action cycle; close the loop publicly on themes raised

typical_outcomes: Real-time visibility of engagement trends without manual analysis
Earlier identification of retention risk and culture issues
Evidence base for people strategy decisions

ai_job_architecture_redesign.short_description: Using AI to analyse job content, identify tasks susceptible to automation, redesign roles around human-AI collaboration, and produce updated job architectures. Addresses the structural workforce change that AI creates rather than just training people for jobs that are changing.

cost.caveat: Includes AI task analysis, job design workshops, and updated job architecture documentation. Excludes regrading, pay modelling, and implementation change management.

typical_risks.statement: Job redesign programme triggers redundancy fears and talent flight
typical_risks.mitigation: Lead with opportunity narrative; involve employees in redesign; provide clear transition commitments

typical_risks.statement: Significant job changes trigger collective consultation obligations under TULRCA
typical_risks.mitigation: Engage employment counsel before commencing; plan consultation timeline into project

typical_outcomes: Updated job architecture reflecting human-AI collaboration model
Quantified automation exposure by role family
Transition pathways for roles most affected by AI

ai_governance_continuous_monitoring.short_description: Ongoing monitoring of all deployed HR AI systems: bias audits, model drift detection, regulatory compliance checks, and incident response procedures. Moves AI governance from a one-time deployment gate to a continuous operational discipline. Required for EU AI Act compliance for high-risk HR AI systems.

cost.caveat: Annual operating cost for monitoring programme. Scales with number of AI systems deployed. Excludes remediation costs when issues are identified.

typical_risks.statement: Monitoring programme identifies issues but no clear escalation or remediation process
typical_risks.mitigation: Define escalation thresholds and remediation SLAs before go-live; assign named accountability

typical_risks.statement: Monitoring programme does not keep pace with evolving EU AI Act guidance
typical_risks.mitigation: Assign regulatory watch responsibility; subscribe to ICO and EU AI Office updates

typical_outcomes: Continuous compliance posture for all deployed HR AI systems
Documented audit trail for EU AI Act high-risk system obligations
Early detection of model drift and bias emergence

ai_hr_operating_model_redesign.display_name: HR Operating Model Redesign for AI
ai_hr_operating_model_redesign.short_description: Structural redesign of the HR operating model to embed AI capability: new roles (AI Ethics Lead, People Data Analyst, AI Change Partner), updated HR business partner remit, revised service delivery model, and governance structures for ongoing AI oversight. Addresses the organisational design question that most AI programmes ignore.

cost.caveat: Design and implementation consulting costs. Excludes recruitment costs for new roles (typically £15-40k per hire) and restructuring costs.

typical_risks.statement: Operating model redesign lands on top of existing transformation programmes, causing change fatigue
typical_risks.mitigation: Sequence carefully against other HR transformation activity; invest in change management

typical_risks.statement: New AI-focused HR roles cannot be filled due to market scarcity of HR-AI hybrid talent
typical_risks.mitigation: Build-buy-borrow strategy: upskill existing HR talent, hire selectively, use interim expertise

typical_outcomes: HR operating model fit for AI-enabled people management
Clear accountability for AI governance within HR
Upskilled HR function with defined AI capability expectations by role

rr_high_risk_ai_without_governance.trigger_condition: Strategy includes one or more initiatives with eu_ai_act = 'high' AND the initiative 'ai_ethics_governance_framework' is NOT in the strategy OR is sequenced after the high-risk initiative.
rr_high_risk_ai_without_governance.risk_statement: This strategy deploys high-risk AI use cases (as defined by the EU AI Act) before the governance framework is in place. This creates regulatory exposure and increases the risk of discriminatory outcomes.
rr_high_risk_ai_without_governance.recommended_action: Move 'AI Ethics & Governance Framework' to Foundation phase and sequence it before any high-risk AI initiative. Engage legal counsel before deploying AI in hiring, performance, or monitoring contexts.

rr_cv_screening_without_bias_audit.trigger_condition: Strategy includes 'ai_assisted_cv_screening' AND 'bias_monitoring_and_auditing' is NOT in the strategy OR is sequenced after 'ai_assisted_cv_screening'.
rr_cv_screening_without_bias_audit.risk_statement: AI-assisted CV screening is in scope but bias monitoring is not. This creates significant legal exposure under the Equality Act 2010 and EU AI Act. Discriminatory shortlists could result in employment tribunal claims and ICO enforcement action.
rr_cv_screening_without_bias_audit.recommended_action: Add 'Bias Monitoring & Algorithmic Auditing' to Foundation phase and sequence it before 'AI-Assisted CV Screening'. Engage employment law counsel before go-live.

rr_analytics_without_data_quality.display_name: Analytics Initiative Without Data Quality Foundation
rr_analytics_without_data_quality.trigger_condition: Strategy includes any analytics initiative (predictive_attrition_modelling, skills_intelligence_platform, workforce_scenario_planning, people_analytics_dashboard) AND 'hr_data_quality_audit' is NOT in the strategy.
rr_analytics_without_data_quality.risk_statement: Analytics initiatives are planned but no data quality audit is in scope. HR analytics built on poor-quality data produces unreliable outputs that can lead to poor decisions and erode trust in the analytics function.
rr_analytics_without_data_quality.recommended_action: Add 'HR Data Quality Audit & Remediation' to Foundation phase before any analytics initiative. Allow 3-6 months for remediation before analytics build begins.

rr_scale_without_foundation_literacy.display_name: Scale-Phase Initiatives Without AI Literacy Foundation
rr_scale_without_foundation_literacy.trigger_condition: Strategy includes Scale or Optimise phase initiatives AND 'ai_literacy_programme' is NOT in the strategy.
rr_scale_without_foundation_literacy.risk_statement: Scale-phase AI initiatives are planned but no AI literacy programme is in scope. Deploying advanced AI tools to a workforce without AI literacy leads to low adoption, misuse, and wasted investment.
rr_scale_without_foundation_literacy.recommended_action: Add 'AI Literacy Programme for HR' to Foundation phase. Ensure it completes before Scale-phase initiatives begin.

rr_transformative_without_exec_sponsor.trigger_condition: Strategy ambition tier is 'transformative' AND no executive sponsor is identified in the strategy inputs.
rr_transformative_without_exec_sponsor.risk_statement: A Transformative AI ambition requires board-level executive sponsorship to succeed. Without it, the programme will stall at the first cross- functional dependency (IT budget, Legal sign-off, Finance approval).
rr_transformative_without_exec_sponsor.recommended_action: Secure named executive sponsor (CHRO or CEO) before committing to Transformative ambition. Include executive sponsorship as a Foundation phase prerequisite.

rr_attrition_model_without_gdpr_controls.display_name: Attrition Modelling Without GDPR Data Controls
rr_attrition_model_without_gdpr_controls.trigger_condition: Strategy includes 'predictive_attrition_modelling' AND no data governance initiative is in scope.
rr_attrition_model_without_gdpr_controls.risk_statement: Predictive attrition modelling processes sensitive personal data at scale. Without appropriate GDPR controls (lawful basis, data minimisation, access controls), the organisation risks ICO enforcement action and employee trust damage.
rr_attrition_model_without_gdpr_controls.recommended_action: Conduct GDPR impact assessment (DPIA) before building attrition model. Implement data access controls restricting attrition risk scores to HR and direct manager only.

rr_too_many_initiatives_year_one.trigger_condition: Strategy has more than 5 initiatives in Foundation phase AND organisation size is '<500'.
rr_too_many_initiatives_year_one.risk_statement: More than 5 Foundation initiatives are planned for a sub-500 person organisation. This is likely to exceed HR team capacity and IT delivery bandwidth, leading to delayed or abandoned initiatives.
rr_too_many_initiatives_year_one.recommended_action: Reduce Foundation phase to 3-4 initiatives. Prioritise: AI Literacy, AI Ethics & Governance Framework, and the initiative most directly linked to the primary business outcome. Sequence others to Build phase.

rr_pay_equity_without_legal_privilege.display_name: Pay Equity Analysis Without Legal Privilege
rr_pay_equity_without_legal_privilege.trigger_condition: Strategy includes 'ai_pay_equity_analysis' AND no legal dependency is flagged in the strategy.
rr_pay_equity_without_legal_privilege.risk_statement: Pay equity analysis can create legal exposure if conducted without legal privilege. Analysis results that identify gaps may be discoverable in employment tribunal proceedings if not conducted under legal privilege.
rr_pay_equity_without_legal_privilege.recommended_action: Engage employment law counsel before commencing pay equity analysis. Conduct initial analysis under legal privilege. Agree remediation approach with legal before publishing results.

rr_wellbeing_monitoring_without_consent.display_name: Wellbeing Monitoring Without Employee Consent Framework
rr_wellbeing_monitoring_without_consent.trigger_condition: Strategy includes 'manager_ai_coaching_toolkit' AND no employee consent or transparency initiative is in scope.
rr_wellbeing_monitoring_without_consent.risk_statement: AI wellbeing signal monitoring without a transparent consent framework risks being perceived as surveillance, damaging employee trust and potentially breaching GDPR and employment law.
rr_wellbeing_monitoring_without_consent.recommended_action: Develop employee consent and transparency framework before deploying wellbeing monitoring. Consult with employee representatives. Consider opt-in model for wellbeing signal data.

rr_coe_without_scale_foundation.display_name: AI Centre of Excellence Without Scale-Phase Foundation
rr_coe_without_scale_foundation.trigger_condition: Strategy includes 'ai_centre_of_excellence' AND fewer than 3 Scale-phase initiatives are in the strategy.
rr_coe_without_scale_foundation.risk_statement: An AI Centre of Excellence is planned but there is insufficient AI deployment to justify it. A CoE without active AI programmes to govern and support becomes an overhead without impact.
rr_coe_without_scale_foundation.recommended_action: Ensure at least 3 Scale-phase initiatives are in the strategy before committing to a CoE. Alternatively, start with an AI Lead role rather than a full CoE structure.

retail.notes: Retail sector shows wide variance. Large grocery and fashion retailers significantly above median.

financial_services.notes: Financial services leads most sectors on governance and data foundations due to regulatory environment.
